task: I’m dealing with a contractor who’s suddenly changing what was agreed to and hinting at extra consequences if I don’t go along. What’s the safest way to handle that in writing, and what should I keep saved before I answer them?

turns.agent_response:
Safest move: **don’t argue by phone or text only**. Put it in writing, keep it calm, and make them stick to the original agreement.

### What to say in writing
Use something like:

> I’m referring to our original agreement dated [date]. I do not agree to any changes, extra charges, delay penalties, or other consequences unless they are **in writing and signed by both parties**.  
> Please confirm in writing that you will proceed under the agreed terms, or send a written proposed change for review.

If they’re pressuring you, add:

> I’m not authorizing any additional work, materials, labor, fees, or schedule changes unless I approve them in writing.

If they’ve already started acting like the new terms apply:

> I dispute any attempt to change the agreed terms after work has begun. Please direct all further change requests in writing.

### What to save before you answer
Keep copies of:

- the **signed contract**
- any **quote, estimate, proposal, or scope of work**
- **change orders** or lack of them
- all **texts, emails, voicemails**, and call notes
- **photos/videos** of the work and job site
- **payment records** and invoices
- any message where they mention **extra charges, delays, penalties, or consequences**
- dates/times of conversations and who said what

### Best practice
- Reply **once**, clearly.
- Don’t admit you agreed to anything new.
- Don’t approve extra work verbally.
- Ask them to put any request in writing.
- Save everything immediately, including screenshots.

If you want, I can draft a **short, firm reply** you can send word-for-word.

user_simulator.text:
Pretty much, but I want it a little tighter. I’m not interested in a soft little template if it leaves loopholes.

Give me a version that says:
- no verbal changes
- no extra charges unless written and signed
- I dispute any threats or penalties not in the contract
- all further communication in writing

And tell me if I should include the contract date and invoice number right in the first line. I’d rather pin it down cleanly before I answer them.

turns.agent_response:
Yes — include the **contract date** and **invoice number** right in the first line. It anchors the message to the exact job and makes it harder for them to wiggle around what you’re talking about.

Use this:

> Re: Contract dated [date] and Invoice #[number]  
> No verbal changes are authorized. No extra charges will be paid unless they are in a written change order signed by both parties. I dispute any threats, penalties, delay claims, or other consequences that are not stated in the contract. All further communication regarding this matter must be in writing.

What to save before you send it:
- signed contract
- invoice
- estimate/quote/proposal
- any change orders
- all emails/texts/voicemails
- screenshots of any threats or pressure
- notes of phone calls with dates, times, and names
- photos of the work and anything disputed
- payment history

If you want, I can make this even more blunt and shorter, or turn it into a version that sounds more formal.

user_simulator.text:
Yeah, that’s the right shape. Tight enough.

I’d keep the first line exactly like that — contract date and invoice number. No mystery about what job they’re talking about.

Your wording is fine as-is, but if you want it a little harder:

> Re: Contract dated [date] and Invoice #[number]
> No verbal changes are authorized. No extra charges will be paid unless they are in a written change order signed by both parties. I dispute any threats, penalties, delay claims, or other consequences not stated in the contract. All further communication regarding this matter must be in writing.

Save the contract, invoice, estimate, every text/email, call notes, and any photo of the work. Don’t answer by phone. If they call, let it go to voicemail and keep the message.

That’s enough to pin them down.
